coredata.dc:title: Broad application prospects near-infrared carbonized polymer dots combined with machine learning for the detection of Cu2+ in seawater and aquatic products
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Rui
You, Jinjie
Chen, Yuge
Zhang, Zeming
Sun, Aili
Liu, Hua
Shi, Xizhi
coredata.dc:description: A highly selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor, using the decision tree (DT) machine learning, was successfully fabricated for the quantitative detection of Cu2+, which is based on near-infrared carbonized polymer dots (r-CPDs). Using o-phenylenediamine and 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid as raw materials first to synthesize the r-CPDs (quantum yield of 32.9%), which pyrrole NH ring can specifically form metal chelates with Cu2+ hiding the electron leap result in the fluorescence burst, by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. Furthermore, the fluorescence sensor based on the r-CPDs was fabricated for ultra-sensitively detect Cu2+ in the range of 0.5–80.0 nM (R2 =0.9986) in aqueous environments and aquatic products with relative standard deviation (RSD) below 4.4% and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.24 nM. Combined with the machine learning algorithm model, the r-CPDs fluorescence color changes accompanied by different Cu2+ concentrations were classified. A self-developed smartphone application equipped with 3D printing technology to prepare portable cartridges successfully applied to rapid real-time detection of trace Cu2+ in various practical samples. The experimental results show that the method has not only convenient for calculating but also accurate. Generally, this study displayed a perfect fusion of r-CPDs fluorescence sensors with machine learning which applied to the determination of Cu2+ in environmental and aquatic products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared
Carbonized polymer dots
Machine learning
Portable instrument
Cu2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of experimental parameters for Cu2+ sensitive detection
Origins and quenching mechanism of r-CPDs
Selectivity and sensitivity evaluation of r-CPDs to Cu2+
The application of DT machine learning-based fluorescent sensor combing a portable UV dark box

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ningbo Municipal Bureau of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo ( 2022S163 ), the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau ( 2021Z056 ), the "Pioneer" and "Leading Goose" R&D Program of Zhejiang ( 2022C02028 , 2023C02038 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Full-scan XPS spectrum of r-CPDs. High-resolution spectra of C 1 s (B), N 1 s (C), and O 1 s (D). (E) Full-scan XPS spectrum of r-CPDs r-CPDs-Cu2+. High-resolution spectra of C 1 s (F), N 1 s (G), and O 1 s (H).
(A) Atomic percentage of elements in r-CPDs. (B) High-resolution spectra of Cu 2p.

DFT calculation of r-CPDs structure models: (A) a benzene ring CQD structure without any group attached, (B) CPDs-1 with CQD attached to one ox-OPD, (C) CPDs-2 with one ox-OPD attached to two CQDs, (C) CPDs-3 with three ox-OPDs and three CQDs attached. (G-L) Calculation of all structures of HOMO and LUMO are shown schematically for energy levels.

(A) and (C) Selectivity of r-CPDs fluorescence detection methods for Cu2+ (B and D) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of r-CPDs in the presence of various interfering substances (B and D are 5 times concentration of interfering ions).
Fluorescence images of r-CPDs with different ions added under (A) daylight (B)365 nm UV light.

(A) Schematic diagram of the smartphone-based sensing platform with the RGB color recognition software used to detect Cu2+. (B) Various assembled parts of the smartphone-based sensing platform. (C) Corresponding pictures obtained under a 365 nm UV lamp versus Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–80.0 nM.
The confusion matrices of (A) seawater (B) Sinonovacula constricta (C) Larimichthys crocea (D) Penaeus vannamei samples.
Based on UV dark box determination of (A) seawater (B) Sinonovacula constricta (C) Larimichthys crocea (D) Penaeus vannamei samples.
Schematic diagrams of the synthesis and detection of Cu2+ by r-CPDs, as well as analysis of fluorescence instrumental detection combined with DT modeling.
r-CPDs was used to determine Cu2+ in actual samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Broad application prospects near-infrared carbonized polymer dots combined with machine learning for the detection of Cu2+ in seawater and aquatic products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Biotechnology of Zhejiang Province, School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, 818 Fenghua Road, Ningbo 315211, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor, using the decision tree (DT) machine learning, was successfully fabricated for the quantitative detection of Cu2+, which is based on near-infrared carbonized polymer dots (r-CPDs). Using o-phenylenediamine and 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid as raw materials first to synthesize the r-CPDs (quantum yield of 32.9%), which pyrrole NH ring can specifically form metal chelates with Cu2+ hiding the electron leap result in the fluorescence burst, by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. Furthermore, the fluorescence sensor based on the r-CPDs was fabricated for ultra-sensitively detect Cu2+ in the range of 0.5–80.0 nM (R2 =0.9986) in aqueous environments and aquatic products with relative standard deviation (RSD) below 4.4% and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.24 nM. Combined with the machine learning algorithm model, the r-CPDs fluorescence color changes accompanied by different Cu2+ concentrations were classified. A self-developed smartphone application equipped with 3D printing technology to prepare portable cartridges successfully applied to rapid real-time detection of trace Cu2+ in various practical samples. The experimental results show that the method has not only convenient for calculating but also accurate. Generally, this study displayed a perfect fusion of r-CPDs fluorescence sensors with machine learning which applied to the determination of Cu2+ in environmental and aquatic products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper, an important metal element in living organisms, is an enzyme cofactor involved in many biological processes in the human body, and also one of the common heavy metals in the aquatic environment. Aquatic organisms are highly sensitive to Cu2+ that teratogenesis occurs in fertilized eggs when fish and amphibians are exposed to 5–10 μg/L of copper [1,2]. In addition, Cu2+ can be enriched in fish muscle and shellfish gonads which will cause irreversible damage if ingestion by humans [3]. Excess Cu2+ can inhibit enzymatic activity leading to cell death while deficient Cu2+ can lead to insufficient synthesis of hemoglobin, elastin and collagen, resulting in anemia, bone abnormalities and other diseases. In addition, dysregulation of Cu2+ homeostasis has been related to pathological conditions such as Wilson's disease, Menke's syndrome, and Alzheimer's disease, which seriously affects human health [4,5]. Therefore, it is particularly important to establish a real-time monitoring method for Cu2+ in the aqueous environment.

The traditional methods, such as ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry (UV-Vis), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) used to detect Cu2+, which are generally limited by the need for complex instrumentation [6,7]. Compared with these methods, fluorescence sensors are more suitable for on-site detection due to their low cost, easy operation, and rapid response [8,9]. Carbon luminescent nanomaterials have shown excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, easy structural modification, high stability, and low cost, which have attracted great attention [10,11]. Carbon luminescent nanomaterials have potential application value in fields such as biomedicine, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, and anti-counterfeiting [12,13]. Due to the interference of self-fluorescence in aquatic organisms (such as fish, shellfish, and algae), will be disturbed by background interference that results in large detection errors [9,10,14,15]. The near-infrared carbon luminescent nanomaterials, as a kind of environment-friendly material, can shield the background interference caused by aquatic organisms. The disadvantage, though, is that low quantum yield (most are at 10%, rarely exceeds 30%) which is caused by the complicated synthesis process and easily quenched by an aqueous environment [11,12,16,17]. In addition, carbon luminescent nanomaterials in the blue and green spectral regions have been widely reported, but the preparation of near-infrared carbon luminescent nanomaterials is difficult. That all greatly limits the application of carbon luminescent nanomaterials in related fields. Carbonized polymer dots are an important branch of carbon luminescent materials, which are prepared by assembling, polymerizing, cross-linking, and then getting through carbonizing small molecules and polymers to form core-shell structures [18]. They not only exhibit some properties of traditional carbon quantum dots but also possess the properties of polymers. Due to the matrix or body's own fluorescence and light scattering, high quantum efficiency, good biocompatibility, and easily prepared near-infrared carbonized polymer dots are currently challenging issue that needs to be addressed for the application of heavy metal pollution detection in actual sample.

Due to the low concentration of target pollutants in the actual detection process, relying solely on curve simulation or linear regression based on a single variable or multiple independent variables (such as fluorescence intensity, fluorescence position, or fluorescence lifetime) as the output variables of a fluorescence sensor cannot effectively measure the relationship between fluorescence changes and concentration. Machine learning based on data analysis can accurately capture subtle changes. When fluorescence variables are combined with machine learning, the detection accuracy of the fluorescence sensor can be greatly improved. So far, machine learning methods used for biosensing detection mainly include Principal Component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), support vector machine (SVM), artificial neural networks (ANNs), random forest (RF), and decision tree (DT), etc [19–24]. The decision tree shows a strong application prospect in fluorescence analysis through carry out complex feature extraction, feature learning and relationship modeling from large data sets. By means of extracting and analyzing the RGB features of images in fluorescence sensors, the decision tree can accurately analyze new variables and provide correct classification, making it suitable for handling diverse fluorescence dynamic datasets [25]. The fluorescence sensors, which rely on a large amount of data support optimized models and provide concentration prediction strategies under different detection conditions, to classify the analytical data when combined with the decision tree model. It can break through the bottleneck of fluorescence sensing detection and realize the rapid analysis and detection of trace heavy metals on site.

This study aims to synthesize red emissive near-infrared carbonized polymer dots (r-CPDs) with high stability and quantum yield through a one-step hydrothermal method using p-phenylenediamine and 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid as precursors. The r-CPDs are then utilized to develop a highly selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor for the quantitative detection of Cu2+ in environments and aquatic products. By combining a decision tree model, the logical relationship between the fluorescence color RGB characteristics of r-CPDs and Cu2+ concentration is accurately determined, enabling the development of a fluorescence sensing system that can rapidly detect trace amounts of Cu2+. A portable UV dark box is fabricated using 3D printing technology to detect trace amounts of Cu2+ in real environments. This fluorescence sensing system, overcoming the limitations of traditional detection methods and offering a promising approach for on-site detection of Cu2+ in the environment and aquatic products, can provide concentration prediction strategies under different detection conditions.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD), 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid (BTC), HgSO4, AgNO3, MnSO4·H2O, MgSO4, CaCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2·4 H2O, CuSO4·5 H2O, KCl, NaCl, ZnSO4·7 H2O, AlCl3, Co(NO3)2, NiSO4, Cr2(SO4)3, NaBr, Na2SO4, Co(NO3)2, NaF were supplied from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade. Dialysis bags (1000 Da) were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ·cm) was filtered with a Youpu water purification system (UPT-II-10 T, Chengdu, China).

The synthesis method of r-CPDs was getting through simple hydrothermal synthesis as shown in Scheme 1. Typical, o-phenylenediamine (10.8 mg, 0.1 mmol) and 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid (21.0 mg, 0.1 mmol) dissolved in deionized water (8.0 mL). After ultrasonication for 5 min, the homogeneous solution was transferred to 15.0 mL polyethylene tetrafluorolined. The sealed reactor was placed in an autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling at room temperature, black dispersions were obtained. Purification was carried out with a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1000) and dialyzed for 24 h to remove all unreacted impurities to obtain a clear solution containing black suspended particulate matter. Finally, the mixture was freeze-dried to obtain black particles called r-CPDs and weighed and redissolved in ethanol to obtain a known concentration of r-CPDs solution, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4.0 °C for subsequent experiments.

The absorption spectra of r-CPDs were recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3300, Mapada, China). The fluorescence spectrum was displayed on the fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). The morphological information of r-CPDs was obtained through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (H-4650, Japan). Surface functionalization of the r-CPDs was determined by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) (Nicolet 6700 USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS) (ESCALAB 250XiX, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yield were obtained by FL3–111 (Hitachi, Japan). Zeta potential measurements were obtained by SurPASS 3 (Anton Paar, Austria). The electrochemical workstation uses CHI6601, which was purchased from Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, China. The 3D printed dark box model was designed by Autodesk 3D Studio Max (3dsmax) version 2019 software.

The prepared 20.0 μL of r-CPDs (40 mg/mL) were dispersed in 1960.0 μL of a specific solvent (ethanol: water, v-v, 8:2), and then 20.0 μL drops of the solution to be tested were added. After the reaction for 3 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of the emission wavelength of r-CPDs were recorded at 560 nm-700 nm under the excitation wavelength of 535 nm, and the slit widths of emission and excitation were 2 nm. fluorescence was also collected under the same conditions, and photographs were taken for smartphone analysis. Selectivity and anti-interference experiments were performed in the above detection system, where 50.0 nM Cu2+ and 250.0 nM interferents cations. The fluorescence emission intensity of r-CPDs with and without metal ions at 595 nm and 645 nm was recorded. The fluorescence images were then taken under a 365 nm UV lamp. All experiments were processed in triplicate.

Seawater samples were collected from the offshore waters of Ningbo and stored in glass bottles. The water samples were filtered using a 0.22 µm membrane to remove particulate matter and microorganisms and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use. Meanwhile, Sinonovacula constricta, Larimichthys crocea and Penaeus vannamei samples were obtained from the local market. Firstly, 4.0 g of samples were placed in a 50.0 mL centrifuge tube, 30.0 mL of ultrapure water and 10.0 mL of acetonitrile were sequentially added, and vortexed for 2 min. Subsequently, 2.0 g of NaCl was added and sonicated for 3 min. Finally, the samples were centrifuged at 1 × 103 g for 10 min, the water layers were collected and placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis.

The images were taken with a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp, and a database was constructed by extracting the fluorescent colors of the actual samples with a self-developed RGB extractor and converting them to RGB values. The database contains the RGB values of 350 samples and their concentrations, in addition, the RGB feature values are randomly divided into a training set and a test set, in which the test set accounts for 30% of the total dataset. Using Python 3.9.13 and Sklearn, Decision tree supervised learning (DT) classified the RGB feature values and analyzed concentrations. A Cu2+ concentration RGB model was developed and Precision, Recall and F-score were used as metrics to comment on the performance of the model. DT is used in the experiments, and the source code of RGB extraction source function and model function are attached to the support files, as detailed in Text S1.

Metrics are necessary to assess the performance of the model and three metrics were used in this study as follows:
(1)
ACC =
TP + TN
TP + FP + FN + TN
(2)
precision =
TP
TP + FP
(3)
recall =
TP
TP + FN

Accuracy is a measure of the performance of a model in terms of how well it correctly predicts the class or value of the target variable. True Positives, True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP) and False Negatives (FN) refer to the number of true positive, true negative, false positive and false negative predictions made by the model, respectively.

The portable UV dark box, imaging module, power supply box, sample box, and printing material are black resin. The overall dimensions are 126.7 mm (L) x 106.0 mm (W) x 87.5 mm (H), as detailed in the specific dimensions. The output voltage of the mobile power supply is 5 V. UV light source is used as UV light-emitting diode (LED) (maximum power is 4 W; λ ex = 365 nm). The smartphone is fixed in the UV dark box, which allows easy access to fluorescence photos of the samples. The fluorescence pictures were then converted to RGB values by using a self-designed smartphone application, and the source code of the application was attached to the support file to quickly and accurately display the specific concentrations by comparison with established data.

r-CPDs were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis of OPD and BTC, the reaction time and ratio of precursors can affect the ratio of nitrogen and oxygen of r-CPDs and thus change the fluorescence intensity, which is crucial for their sensitivity. Therefore, the molar ratio of OPD and BTC, the reaction time and their synthesis pH conditions were optimized to ensure the high selectivity of r-CPDs. As displayed in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged after 5 h, and the ratio of OPD to BTC at 1:1 had the best fluorescence quenching effect on Cu2+. Furthermore, r-CPDs surface carboxyl and amino groups were dehydrated and condensed to form peptide bonds, and the pH would affect the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency. Therefore, the pH of 3.0–10.0 was selected to observe the fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs.

The morphology and size of r-CPDs were analyzed by TEM. As shown in Fig. S2A, r-CPDs are uniformly dispersed in solution with an average diameter of 5.0 nm. HRTEM images showed that the lattice spacing of r-CPDs was 0.22 nm (Fig. S2B). Zeta potential showed that the surface charge of r-CPDs was positive at 6.11 mV (Fig. S2C). Furthermore, FTIR was used to study the functional group types of r-CPDs. Fig. S2D shows the telescopic vibrational peaks of -NH2 and -OH at 3440 cm−1 in CQDs, = C-H at 2930 cm−1, CO and N-H at 1725 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1, respectively, and C-H at 1480 cm−1 and 1220 cm−1, respectively, and C-N vibrational peaks at 1480 cm−1 and 1220 cm−1, respectively [26–28]. The functional groups containing carbon and nitrogen originate from the condensation reaction between OPD and BTC, followed by carbonation, and the hydroxyl groups originate from BTC as well as OH- in solution.

XPS characterization was performed to determine the elemental composition and chemical bonding type of r-CQDs. As elucidated in Fig. 1 A, the elemental composition percentages of r-CPDs were those of C (77.14%), O (13.77%) and N (9.10%). In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s convolved at 289.67, 287.46, 285.17, 284.39, and 283.26 eV as five peaks ( Fig. 2B) corresponding to CN, CO, C-O, C-N, CC, and C-C bonds, respectively [29]. The high resolution XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. 2 C) showed two peaks corresponding to at 398.76 eV and 397.55 eV, corresponding to secondary amine (-NH-) and pyridinic-N [30]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s corresponds to 531.76 eV and 530.20 eV, corresponding to C-O-C/C-OH. The above results combined with the FTIR spectroscopy results further confirm the nitrogen doping in r-CPDs and the modification of the surface of r-CPDs with functional groups such as C-N, CN and N-H.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental parameters for Cu2+ sensitive detection
ce:section.ce:para: Stability and optical properties are important parameters in the detection of carbon quantum dot organic frameworks. Therefore, the stability of carbon quantum dots organic frameworks to solvent, water content and pH was investigated before testing different metal ions. As shown in Fig. S3, r-CPDs have better fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency compared to other solvents using pure ethanol, indicating that r-CPDs are optimal dispersed in ethanol. Considering the application in actual water samples, the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency of r-CPDs were further investigated for water content and acid-base conditions. As shown in Fig. S3B and S3C, the maximum value was reached at 20% of water content, and the quenching efficiency of r-CPDs decreased with further increase of water content, which was related to the polarity of water and caused aggregation quench. 595 nm and 645 nm reached the maximum value at pH 7.0. The reason may be ascribed to that r-CPDs have a large number of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface, which form hydrogen bonds that lead to r-CPDs quenched under acidic conditions, and under alkaline conditions, the electron leap of functional groups on the surface of r-CPDs may be affected, resulting in a decrease in r-CPDs fluorescence intensity [31]. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. S3E, in the 0–30‰ range of salinity, there is no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs and on the detection of Cu2+. Therefore, in the actual sample assay, a mixture of ethanol and water (v: v, 8:2) was used as a solvent with pH 7.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origins and quenching mechanism of r-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: A strong near-infrared fluorescence quantum yield of synthetic r-CPDs in ethanol reached 32.9%. DFT theoretical analysis of the possible structural model energy levels of r-CPDs further confirms the local structure of r-CPDs with high stability of red light (Fig. S4). Fig. 3 A–D shows the optimized model of the CQD core without any group modification (CQD), ox-OPD connected to 1 CQD (CPDs-1), one ox-OPD connected to 2 CQDs (CPDs −2), and a ring structure (CPDs −3) formed by 3 ox-OPDs and 3 CQDs connected. The energy levels of all models were calculated for comparison [32,33]. The structural models of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels for the ground and excited states and the energy level diagrams are shown in Fig. 3 E–L. The electron cloud density distribution and the degree of delocalization of the entire molecular HOMO and LUMO structures are determined. The change in ΔE in Table S1. indicates that the connection of ox-OPDs and CQDs affects the central electron cloud and the energy levels of the triplet excited states change significantly. From CPDs-1 to CPDs-3, ΔE(S0/T1) decreases from 1.716 eV to the minimum value of 1.660 eV, indicating that the connection of ox-OPD and CQDs significantly diminishes the energy level of the triple-excited state and stabilizes the orbital jump from the singlet to the triplet state to produce long-wavelength emission. In summary, the structure of the organic framework formed by joining ox-OPDs with CQDs plays an important role in reducing ΔE(S0/T1), which is consistent with the results of red fluorescence emission from high fluorescence quantum yield.

As Fig. 2E-H demonstrates the changes in the elemental composition and chemical bonding of r-CPDs after the addition of Cu2+, where C1s and N1s show slight shifts. The Fig. 2 F was shown a significant increase in the binding energies of C-O/CO (288.75 eV), C-N (286.48 eV) and CN (291.50 eV) in C1s, Fig. 2 G shows a 2.34 eV increase in the binding energy of pyrrole N (401.10 eV) in N1s, producing a large chemical shift. And as Fig. 2H shows that the chemical C-O (533.54 eV) peak in O1s increases. This combination indicates that the O and N elements of the r-CPDs lose electrons. In Fig. 1B, the XPS spectra of r-CPDs-Cu correspond to 933.14 eV and 930.97 eV, corresponding to Cu2+ 2p3/2 and Cu+ 2p3/2, respectively with the binding energies of Cu2+ 2p3/2 and Cu+ 2p3/2 at 935.00 eV and 933.00 eV, respectively, reported by Goyal et al. and He et al. with a downward lateral shift of which corresponds to the binding energy change of -NH- and pyridinic-N, further confirming the coordination interactions [34,35].

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S5A, as the excitation wavelength increases or decreases, the fluorescence emission peaks of r-CPDs only show a decrease in fluorescence intensity but no significant change in wavelength, indicating that r-CPDs do not have a fluorescence excitation dependence, which arises from their relatively homogeneous size and surface passivation of CQDs, which provide a narrow distribution of surface energy states [36]. In Fig. S5B, DPV shows that r-CPDs have two redox peaks accepting electron transduction, and after the addition of Cu2+, the oxidation peak of pyrrole N diminishes, while a new copper amine-mediated oxidation peak appears. UV-absorbance results showed that the r-CPDs have two absorption peaks centered at 536 nm and 570 nm as illustrated in Fig. S5C. The addition of Cu2+ into the r-CPDs solution produces an absorption band centered at 440 nm, while there is almost no absorption at 536 nm and 570 nm. As illustrated in Fig. S5D, the lifetime of red emission is 2.18 ns, the fluorescence lifetime of added Cu2+ is almost unchanged, and the emission lifetimes of Cu at 20.0, 50.0 and 100.0 nM are 2.94, 3.02 and 2.93 ns, respectively, which indicates that a static fluorescence burst mechanism occurs. The surface of the prepared r-CPDs is rich in amino groups, so the two new absorption bands are attributed to the formation of copper amines by Cu2+ with its surface amino groups. Furthermore, the cause of FL burst is the formation of copper amines that fill the sp2 structural domain defect formed by the amino groups of r-CPDs, which burst them through the internal filtration effect [37,38]. To further verify that the cause of burst inactivation of r-CPDs by Cu2+ is due to the filling of structural defects formed by amino acids, control experiments were performed with OPD without BTC, CQDs without sp2 defects (Fig. S5D and S5F). the results showed that the fluorescence of CQDs was not affected by the presence of Cu2+, and in addition the chelation between Cu2+ and r-CPDs could be eliminated by the competitive effect of adding lysine copperamine-associated endofiltration effect that restoring of with the fluorescence burst [39]. In a word, the r-CPD fluorescence is reversible, due to the structural defects in amino acid formation competition and lysine internal filtration associated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity evaluation of r-CPDs to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluating the performance of the r-CPDs, selective and competitive experiments with various metal ions were performed. As illustrated in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of Cu2+ was only one-third of that of r-CPDs, while the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of other metal ions (Cother = 5 C Cu2+) was the same as that of r-CPDs, as well as almost the same intensity (RSD <2.8%) as in Figs. 4B and 4E without the addition of metal ions. Moreover, as demonstrated in Fig. 5, among the other metal ions and anions added, only Cu2+ was significantly burst and the red fluorescence was diminished under 365 nm UV light, indicating that r-CPDs can be burst by Cu2+ and have high selectivity.

The anti-interference ability of r-CPDs was further evaluated by adding various types of interfering ions in a competitive experiment where Cu2+ as the blank. As can be seen from Fig. 4B and D, the fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs after the addition of metal ion interfering substances is almost the same as that in the presence of Cu2+ alone (F0/F). This may be due to the fact that other metal ions cannot compete with Cu2+ for the binding sites of r-CPDs and some metal ions can form metal chelates with NH rings. However, their energy level mismatch limits the electron hopping ability and does not affect the return of excited state electrons to the ground state orbital of the NH ring. Therefore, the established sensing system of r-CPDs has good selectivity and anti-interference ability for Cu2+.

In order to improve accurate identification and quantitative performance, a method combining machine learning techniques was carried out. As shown in the Fig. S6, the RGB features of r-CPDs for Cu2+ at 0.5–80.0 nM concentrations were extracted, and a regression model between the concentration and the RGB data of the fluorescence sensor was established. As illustrated in the Fig. S7, the Cu2+ concentrations were arranged from low to high by RGB feature variations into ten groups (0 to 9). Tree-based Pipeline Optimization Tool (TPOT) training and optimization were performed for each group model and studied by 10-fold cross-validation method. Three main evaluation metrics, Precision, Recall and F-score, were selected to assess the regression performance of the models. As depicted in Table S2 and S3, all 350 samples were trained, where 30% of the sample size was selected as validation samples (105 samples), and high accuracy was obtained for all groups, with R2 values optimized to 0.8300–1.0000, indicating that the model fits well with data of different concentrations. The established RGB feature value grouping is shown in Fig. S8, which is divided into 10 classification models based on the R, G, and B range values by DT model to deal with the fluorescence color variation of r-CPDs at different concentrations of Cu2+.

To better illustrate the predictive performance of the regression model, the results are represented visually by receiver operating characteristic curves (ROCs). As displayed in Fig. S9, a number of different critical values were set for the continuous variables, thus calculating a series of true and false positive rates, which were then plotted as a curve with the false positive rate as the vertical coordinate and the true rate as the horizontal coordinate; the larger the area under the curve, the higher the diagnostic accuracy in Fig. S9 and Fig. S10. On the ROC curve, the point closest to the upper left of the coordinate plot is the critical value where both the false positive rate and the true rate are high, where all concentrations of Cu2+ were obtained with high accuracy, where R2 remained between 0.95 and 1, and the minimum value of R2 was 0.9280.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of DT machine learning-based fluorescent sensor combing a portable UV dark box
ce:section.ce:para: The linearity, sensitivity, accuracy and precision of the fluorescence sensor were investigated under optimal conditions. To obtain linear fitting curves of fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs versus Cu2+ concentration and fast response accuracy time, linear curves of relative fluorescence intensity (I0/I) versus Cu2+ concentration were plotted, where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs without and with Cu2+, respectively. As shown in Fig. S11A, the fluorescence intensity changes from 0–150 s when 80.0 nM of Cu2+ is added to the r-CPDs solution, and the fluorescence intensity of r-CPDs will reach the lowest at 2 min, indicating that the r-CPDs reach response saturation with Cu2+. As reflected in Fig. S11B and S11C, the I0/I has excellent linearity with Cu2+ in the concentration range of 0.5–80.0 nM. The fitted linear equations can be described as y = 0.87147 + 0.12261 x (R2 = 0.9986). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.24 nM (RSD =1.06%) according to the equation LOD= 3σ/K (n = 11), where σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity measured in blank and K is the slope of the calibration curve. New optical sensors based on non-toxic carbon materials, such as carbon dots, aim to reduce the use of toxic heavy metals like CdSe, ZnS, and CdSeTe. Carbon dots possess excellent fluorescence properties, high light stability, water solubility, and favorable bio-compatibility, making them promising candidates for optical sensing applications [40]. The utilization of longer wavelength near-infrared light in these materials offers significant advantages for future biological applications [41]. Table S11 summarizes the main work used for copper ion sensing, including Material name, Color, QY (%), Detection sample, Detection range (nM), LOD (nM), and Recovery (%).

To further improve the detection efficiency and realize the portable field inspection, the specific dimensions of the portable UV dark box were fabricated. The dark box is designed with a 365 nm UV light source, a sample pool, and a visualization window. This configuration allows for convenient color change reading using the smartphone without any interference from external lighting conditions or manipulation (Fig. S12). Correspondingly, the readable fluorescent image application was designed and trained with 350 fluorescence images of the previous samples shot by the Android phone ( Fig. 6). For evaluating the predictive performance of this testbed for Cu2+, each independent sample of seawater, Sinonovacula constricta, Larimichthys crocea and Penaeus vannamei samples were selected. As illustrated in Fig. 7 and Table S2-S9, all 269 samples tested were correctly separated in the seawater samples. They all lie on the diagonal of the matrix, which means that the predicted categories of the 269 test samples match the actual categories, with misclassifications of 4, 2 and 1 occurring in groups 1, 2 and 3 in Fig. 7A. Four 0.1 nM Cu2+ concentrations were classified as 0.5 nM concentrations, two 0.5 nM Cu2+ concentrations were classified as 1.0 nM concentrations and one 1.0 nM Cu2+ concentration was classified as 2.0 nM concentration.

The model provided excellent accuracy in predicting different independent samples. The spiked recoveries of the actual samples were further analyzed. As summarized in Table 1, the recoveries of Cu2+ were ranging from 84.7% to 109.4% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) not exceeding 4.4%. For the study, we have developed a smartphone application that includes four different algorithm models for different samples. When conducting real-life sample testing, users can select the appropriate algorithm model within the program. By using the smartphone camera to capture images, applying intelligent color extraction, and analyzing the colors with different algorithm models, it is possible to obtain actual concentration data specific to different samples. As shown in Fig. 8, the ultraviolet dark box we designed and modeled in-house and fabricated using 3D printing technology, together with the smartphone application to form a portable device. This device has been successfully applied to actual samples (such as seawater, Sinonovacula constricta, Larimichthys crocea and Penaeus vannamei samples) that demonstrating high accuracy. The recoveries of the portable UV dark box-based sensor in real samples ranged from 91.2 to 137.3% with a lower LOD of 0.47 nM (RSD=3.42%), which confirmed the high reproducibility and reliability of the combined UV dark box and smartphone assay for the detection of Cu2+ in real samples. The percentage error in the evaluation of the detection limit was recalculated and the data was given in the Electronic Supplementary Information Fig S14 and Table S10. The above results demonstrated that the fluorescence sensor based on the near-infrared r-CPDs combined with DT machine learning and a portable UV dark box exhibited good analytical performance and could realize the ultrasensitive detection of Cu2+ residues in seawater and aquatic products. That is suitable for the rapid, convenient, and real-time detection of various practical samples.

A fluorescent sensor based on r-CPDs combined with a machine learning approach was developed for the quantitative detection of trace Cu2+ and the use of a UV dark box to achieve instant on-site detection of Cu2+. One-step hydrothermal synthesis of r-CPDs from o-phenylenediamine and 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylic acid with a high quantum yield of 32.9%. DFT theory demonstrates that the chain-connected conjugated structure in r-CPDs is the key to lowering the energy level of the triplet excited state, resulting in the ability to have a high charge mobility with a high quantum yield. The prepared r-CPDs fluorescence sensor exhibited a good linear relationship between 0.5 and 80 nM (R2 =0.9986), low LOD (0.24 nM), and satisfactory recovery rates (84.7% to 109.4%). The relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 4.38%. The combination of DT machine learning technology and portable UV dark box with r-CPDs fluorescence sensor further improved detection efficiency, achieved on-site detection of Cu2+, and achieved good recovery rates (91.17–137.33%) and lower LOD (0.47 nM) in aquatic product detection. This method is suitable for the on-site detection of Cu2+ in the environment and aquatic products.

Shi Xizhi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision. Liu Hua: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Sun Aili: Investigation, Supervision, Validation. You Jinjie: Data curation, Investigation, Resources. Chen Rui: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhang Zeming: Investigation, Supervision, Validation. Chen Yuge: Investigation, Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo (2022S163), the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2021Z056), the "Pioneer" and "Leading Goose" R&D Program of Zhejiang (2022C02028, 2023C02038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.135245.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rui Chen received his bachelor's degree from Yibin University in 2021. He is currently studying for his Master's degree at Ningbo University. His research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence and colorimetric sensors and their applications in the environment and organisms.

Jinjie You received his Master's degree from Ningbo University in 2022. He is currently studying for his PhD at Ningbo University. His research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence and colorimetric sensors and their applications in the environment and organisms.

Yuge Chen received his bachelor's degree from Xingzhi College of Zhejiang Normal University in 2021. He is currently studying for a master's degree at the Marine College of Ningbo University. His main research interests are the construction of molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors and their applications in the detection of environmental pollutants.

Zeming Zhang received his BS and Ph.D degree from Ocean University of China in 2013 and 2018. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the detection, migration and transformation of organic pollutants.

Aili Sun received her Ph.D degree from Ningbo University in 2017. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the development and application of fluorescence sensors for environment and food safety.

Hua Liu received her Ph.D degree from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2019. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the development of nano-fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for environment and organisms.

Xizhi Shi received his Ph.D degree from Shanghai Jiaotong University in 2008. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the development of fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for bioanalysis.
